questions.question: What is the difference between KBC commands and KBD commands?

options.A: There is no difference; both use port 0x60.
options.B: KBC commands (written to 0x64) configure the KBC itself; KBD commands (forwarded via 0xD4) configure the keyboard.
options.C: KBC commands configure the keyboard; KBD commands configure the KBC.
options.D: KBD commands are only available in PS/2 mode.

questions.explanation: KBC commands are sent to the keyboard controller (i8042) itself via port 0x64. KBD commands are addressed to the keyboard controller chip at the keyboard (C@KBD) — they must be forwarded by writing to port 0x60 after sending command 0xD4 to the KBC.

questions.question: What is an anonymous struct inside a union in C?

options.B: A struct within a union that has no tag or variable name; its members are accessed directly as if they were union members.
options.C: A struct that cannot be printed.
options.D: A struct initialised to zero by default.

questions.explanation: An anonymous struct inside a union has no name. Its members are accessible directly, as if they belonged to the enclosing union. This is used in reg86_t to allow access to x86 registers at 8-, 16-, and 32-bit granularity.

questions.question: What is the i8254 Mode 2 (Rate Generator) behaviour?

options.A: OUT goes high when count reaches 0 and stays high.
options.B: OUT goes low for one clock cycle when count reaches 0, then the counter reloads and repeats.
options.C: OUT alternates high/low with a 50% duty cycle.
options.D: OUT goes low once and stays low until a hardware trigger.

questions.explanation: Mode 2 (Rate Generator/Divide-by-N): the counter decrements; when it reaches 0, OUT goes low for exactly one clock pulse, then the counter reloads automatically and the cycle repeats — generating a periodic pulse train.

questions.question: How many IRQ lines does the PC's original PIC architecture support in total?
questions.explanation: The cascaded PIC setup (PIC1 + PIC2) provides 15 usable IRQ lines (IRQ 0–7 on PIC1, IRQ 8–15 on PIC2, minus IRQ2 which is used for the cascade). Modern systems use the APIC with more lines.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly declares and uses a C enum?

options.A: enum Color {RED, GREEN, BLUE}; Color c = RED;
options.B: enum Color {RED=0, GREEN=1, BLUE=2}; enum Color c = RED;

questions.explanation: The standard C way is to declare: enum Color { RED=0, GREEN=1, BLUE=2 }; and use: enum Color c = RED; (In C, you must write 'enum Color' as the type name; in C++ you can omit 'enum'.)

questions.question: In which address space are the VBE and i8254 registers located?

options.B: Virtual address space of the process.
options.C: I/O address space (accessed via IN/OUT instructions).

questions.explanation: The i8254 (ports 0x40–0x43), KBC (0x60, 0x64), RTC (0x70–0x71), and similar I/O controllers are mapped in the I/O address space and accessed via the IN/OUT assembly instructions (or sys_inb/sys_outb in Minix).

questions.question: In the two-dimensional state machine dispatch table void (*eht[][])(void), how do you call the handler for state st and event ev?

options.B: (*eht[st][ev])();

questions.explanation: The table is indexed first by state (st), then by event (ev): (*eht[st][ev])() dereferences and calls the appropriate handler function pointer for the given state/event combination.

questions.question: What is the range of periodic interrupt periods the RTC can generate (using RS0–RS3 in REGISTER_A)?

options.B: 122 µs to 0.5 s

questions.explanation: The RS0–RS3 bits in REGISTER_A select the periodic interrupt rate from a range of 122 µs (fastest) to 500 ms (slowest). A rate selector value of 0 disables periodic interrupts.

questions.question: What does the | operator do in C?

options.A: Logical OR — returns true if either operand is non-zero.
options.B: Bitwise OR — sets a bit if it is set in either operand.
options.C: Exclusive OR — sets a bit if it differs between operands.

questions.explanation: The | operator is bitwise OR: each bit of the result is 1 if the corresponding bit is 1 in at least one of the operands. Used to set specific bits: flags |= BIT(k).

questions.question: What does the ^ operator do in C?

options.B: Power/exponentiation.
options.C: Bitwise XOR — sets a bit if it differs between operands.

questions.explanation: The ^ operator is bitwise XOR (exclusive OR). Each bit of the result is 1 if the corresponding bits of the two operands differ. Used to toggle bits: flags ^= BIT(k).

questions.question: In the AnimSprite class example, why is va_end() important?

options.A: It frees the memory allocated for the variable argument list.
options.B: It is required to finalise access to the variable argument list; failing to call it causes undefined behaviour.
options.C: It signals the end of the sprite animation.
options.D: It closes the file descriptor used for the animation.

questions.explanation: va_end() must be called to clean up the va_list state. On some architectures, failing to call va_end() causes undefined behaviour (e.g., corrupted stack frames). It is the mandatory counterpart to va_start().

questions.question: In xpm_load(), what does the XPM_INDEXED type parameter specify?

options.A: The XPM is stored in an indexed binary format.
options.B: The output pixmap uses the Minix default palette indices (for VBE mode 0x105).
options.C: The XPM uses indexed memory allocation.
options.D: The function should use direct colour encoding.

questions.explanation: XPM_INDEXED tells xpm_load() to produce a pixmap where each pixel is a 1-byte palette index compatible with the Minix default colour palette in VBE mode 0x105 (8-bit indexed/packed-pixel mode).

questions.question: In Minix 3, when Minix already has a Timer 0 interrupt handler installed, what happens when a student DD subscribes to IRQ 0?

options.A: The student's IH replaces Minix's IH.
options.B: The student's IH is ignored.
options.C: Both Minix's IH and the student's DD are notified — interrupt sharing.

questions.explanation: Minix supports interrupt sharing on IRQ lines. When a DD subscribes IRQ 0 without IRQ_EXCLUSIVE, both Minix's built-in Timer 0 handler and the DD receive the interrupt notification.

questions.question: What is the purpose of calloc() vs malloc() in C?

options.A: calloc() allocates memory for an array and initialises all bytes to zero; malloc() does not initialise.
options.B: calloc() is faster; malloc() is more portable.
options.C: calloc() can allocate more than 4 GB; malloc() cannot.
options.D: calloc() is for structs; malloc() is for arrays.

questions.explanation: calloc(nmemb, size) allocates nmemb * size bytes and zero-initialises all of them. malloc(size) allocates size bytes without initialisation. calloc is useful when you need a zeroed buffer.

questions.question: In VRAM linear mode, what is BytesPerScanLine in the vbe_mode_info_t struct?

options.A: The number of pixels per horizontal line.
options.B: The number of bytes per horizontal line, which may be larger than XResolution * bytes_per_pixel due to alignment.
options.C: The total number of bytes in VRAM.

questions.explanation: BytesPerScanLine is the number of bytes from the start of one scan line to the start of the next. It may be larger than XResolution * BitsPerPixel/8 due to horizontal stride padding. Always use this value rather than computing width * bpp to find the start of a row.

questions.question: If a mouse supports scroll wheel (as in the enhanced IntelliMouse protocol), what is the minimum number of bytes needed per packet?
questions.explanation: The standard PS/2 mouse packet is 3 bytes. Adding scroll wheel support (IntelliMouse Extension) adds a 4th byte for wheel data, requiring 4 bytes minimum.

questions.question: In C, what is the correct way to check if flag bit 5 is set in variable 'status'?

options.B: if (status & BIT(5))

questions.explanation: status & BIT(5) produces a non-zero value if and only if bit 5 is set in status. Comparing with == would only be true if no other bits are set. The | operator sets bit 5 rather than testing it.

questions.question: When reading from the KBC output buffer (port 0x60) during polling, which bits of the status register must be checked before reading?

options.B: OBF must be set AND Parity and Timeout error bits must be clear.

questions.explanation: Before reading from port 0x60, check: (1) OBF=1 (data available), (2) Parity and Timeout bits = 0 (no communication error). If there is an error, read and discard the byte.

questions.question: For Timer 2 in Mode 3 with binary counting and initial value 0x04D2, what is the control word written to port 0x43?

options.B: 0xB6

questions.explanation: Control word: SC1SC0=10 (timer 2), RW1RW0=11 (LSB then MSB), M2M1M0=011 (mode 3), BCD=0 (binary) → 10 11 011 0 = 1011 0110 = 0xB6.

questions.question: To read the RTC Register A, what sequence of I/O operations is needed?

options.B: sys_outb(0x70, RTC_REG_A); sys_inb(0x71, &val);

questions.explanation: RTC access is always two steps: (1) write the target register address to port 0x70 (RTC_ADDR_REG), then (2) read the register value from port 0x71 (RTC_DATA_REG).

questions.question: Which of the following format specifiers is used in printf to print a string?

options.B: %d

questions.explanation: %s in printf prints a null-terminated string (char*). %c prints a single character, %d a signed integer, %p a pointer address.

questions.question: Why should vbe_get_mode_info() be called only once and its results cached?

options.A: The function is very slow and uses a lot of CPU time.
options.B: It must allocate a struct in the first 1 MB of physical address space each time, and repeated calls may fail.
options.C: The BIOS can only handle one VBE call per session.

questions.explanation: vbe_get_mode_info() relies on BIOS INT 10h which uses real-mode memory in the first 1 MB. This allocation can fail if called repeatedly. Store the result in static variables and use them throughout the program.

questions.question: To set pixel at (x, y) in a screen with bytes_per_pixel = 2 (16-bit colour), the correct formula for the VRAM offset is:

options.B: (y * vres + x) * 2

questions.explanation: Pixels are stored row-major in linear mode. Row y starts at y * hres pixels from the origin, plus x pixels into that row. Multiplied by bytes_per_pixel: (y * hres + x) * bytes_per_pixel.

questions.question: The Aux bit (bit 5) of the KBC Status Register is crucial when both keyboard and mouse are connected. What happens if you read port 0x60 when Aux=1?

options.B: You get mouse data.
options.C: You get a status byte, not data.

questions.explanation: When the Aux bit is set in the KBC Status Register, it means the byte in the output buffer (port 0x60) comes from the mouse (auxiliary device). If Aux=0, the byte is from the keyboard.

questions.question: If IRQ_REENABLE is NOT set in sys_irqsetpolicy(), what must the DD do after handling each interrupt?

options.B: Call sys_irqenable(&hook_id) to unmask the IRQ line in the PIC.
options.D: Call sys_irqdisable(&hook_id) to reset the mask.

questions.explanation: Without IRQ_REENABLE, the GIH leaves the IRQ line masked after the notification. The DD is responsible for unmasking it by calling sys_irqenable(&hook_id) as soon as it is ready to receive the next interrupt.

questions.question: In OOP-in-C, how do you ensure that all methods of a 'class' operate on the same object instance?

options.B: By passing a pointer to the object's state struct as the first argument to each method.
options.C: By storing a static reference to the current object.

questions.explanation: In C, the equivalent of the 'this' pointer is an explicit first argument — a pointer to the struct that represents the object. This is how all LCOM 'class' methods (put_queue, get_queue, etc.) are implemented.

questions.question: What does VGA stand for?

options.B: Video Graphics Adapter

questions.explanation: VGA stands for Video Graphics Adapter (also sometimes Array). It is the IBM standard that defines low-resolution graphics modes (640×480 @ 16 colors, 320×240 @ 256 colors) and the basic video BIOS interface.

questions.question: What is vertical retrace (or vertical blanking interval)?

options.A: The time between two frames during which the display beam returns to the top-left corner.
options.B: The time it takes to draw a single horizontal scan line.
options.C: The frequency of the horizontal sync signal.
options.D: A period when the monitor turns off to save power.

questions.explanation: In a CRT (and analogously in LCD with fixed timing), after the last line of one frame is drawn, there is a brief interval (~500 µs for CRT) during which the beam resets to the top-left corner before the next frame scan begins. This is the vertical retrace / vertical blanking interval.

questions.question: What is Mode 0 (Interrupt on Terminal Count) of the i8254?

options.A: The counter generates a square wave output.
options.B: The OUT pin goes high and remains high when the count reaches 0.
options.C: The OUT pin goes low for one clock cycle when count reaches 0.
options.D: The counter reloads and counts again after reaching 0.

questions.explanation: In Mode 0, the OUT pin is initially low. When the counter reaches 0, OUT goes high and remains high until a new control word is written. This mode is used to count events and generate a single interrupt.

questions.question: What is the effect of the >> right-shift operator on an unsigned integer value?

options.A: Divides by 2 raised to the right operand, with the vacated bits filled with the sign bit.
options.B: Divides by 2 raised to the right operand, with the vacated bits filled with 0 (logical shift).
options.D: The behaviour is undefined for unsigned types.

questions.explanation: For unsigned integers, >> is a logical right shift: vacated bits on the left are filled with 0. For signed integers, the behaviour for filling vacated bits (0 or 1) is implementation-defined.

questions.question: In Lab 3 (kbd_test_timed_scan), the program terminates either on ESC release OR after idle seconds. How are both events handled?

options.A: By subscribing only the keyboard interrupt.
options.B: By subscribing both the keyboard and Timer 0 interrupts, and handling both in the same driver_receive() loop.

questions.explanation: kbd_test_timed_scan() subscribes both Timer 0 (for timeout counting) and keyboard interrupts. In the driver_receive() loop, it checks msg.m_notify.interrupts for both the timer bit and the keyboard bit, handling each appropriately.

questions.question: Why should the DM (Data Mode), 24/12, and DSE bits in RTC REGISTER_B not be changed by a user program?

options.B: Changing them may interfere with how the OS reads the RTC and require re-setting all time registers in the new format.
options.C: The RTC will reset if these bits change.
options.D: These bits control hardware features not accessible by software.

questions.explanation: The DM bit selects BCD vs. binary encoding; 24/12 selects 24-hour vs. 12-hour format. Changing these bits affects how the OS (and BIOS) interprets the time registers. Additionally, changing DM or 24/12 requires rewriting the time/date registers in the new format.

questions.question: When is it advantageous to use an application-dependent interrupt handler vs. an application-independent one?

options.B: Application-independent IH is shorter (less code in the critical path) and reusable; application-dependent is simpler to implement for one-off tasks.
options.C: Application-dependent IH requires kernel support; application-independent does not.

questions.explanation: Application-independent IHs are shorter (only device protocol code), more reusable, and potentially more responsive. Application-dependent IHs tightly couple event detection to handling, which is simpler for one-time projects but not reusable.

questions.question: What does the POSIX function tickdelay() do in Minix?

options.B: Suspends the calling process for a specified number of timer ticks.
options.C: Returns the number of timer ticks since boot.

questions.explanation: tickdelay(int ticks) suspends execution for the specified number of Minix timer ticks. Used in KBC polling loops to wait for the KBC to process a command (e.g., wait up to 20 ms for a response).

questions.question: What does the Null Count flag in the i8254 Read-Back status indicate?

options.A: The counter value is currently zero.
options.B: The counter has not yet loaded the initial counting value after a new control word was written.
options.C: The counter is counting in BCD mode.

questions.explanation: The Null Count flag (bit 6 of the status byte) is set when the counter has received a new count value (via a write to the control register) but has not yet loaded it into the counting element. Reading the count value when Null Count is set may return an invalid value.

questions.question: In VBE mode 0x115 (1280×1024 @ 24-bit RGB), if BitsPerPixel=24 and BytesPerScanLine=3840, what is the offset for pixel at (x=2, y=3)?
questions.explanation: Offset = x*3 + y*BytesPerScanLine = 2*3 + 3*3840 = 6 + 11520 = 11526.

questions.question: In C, what is a pointer?

options.A: A variable that stores the value of another variable.
options.B: A data type whose value is a memory address.

questions.explanation: A C pointer is a variable whose value is a memory address. Pointers allow indirect access to data, dynamic memory allocation, and passing large data structures efficiently (by reference).

questions.question: In the resize_queue() function of the queue implementation, why does the code copy elements from the beginning of the buffer to the new extended area?

options.B: To handle wrap-around in the circular buffer after doubling its size.
options.D: To convert the queue to a stack.

questions.explanation: The queue uses a circular buffer. When the buffer is resized (doubled), elements at indices 0..in-1 (those 'wrapped around' in the old circular buffer) must be copied to positions size..size+in-1 in the new buffer so the circular structure remains correct after the size change.

questions.question: What does mouse command 0xF6 (Set Defaults) do?

options.B: Sets the mouse back to its default values (stream mode, 100 samples/s, 1:1 scaling).

questions.explanation: Command 0xF6 restores the mouse to its factory defaults: stream mode enabled, 100 samples/second, 4 counts/mm resolution, 1:1 scaling. It does not reset the mouse hardware itself (that is 0xFF).

questions.question: When should you prefer uint8_t over unsigned char in C code?

options.A: When you need exactly 8 bits regardless of the platform.
options.B: When you need character input/output.
options.C: When you want a signed byte value.
options.D: uint8_t and unsigned char are always identical.

questions.explanation: uint8_t (from <stdint.h>) is exactly 8 bits wide on all platforms where it is defined. unsigned char is at least 8 bits but could be larger. For I/O register access where exact bit widths matter, uint8_t is the correct choice.

questions.question: A state machine for mouse gesture detection would typically transition between states on which type of events?

options.B: Mouse packet events (movement, button presses/releases).

questions.explanation: A mouse gesture state machine transitions on mouse events: button press, button release, and displacement (movement) events derived from parsed mouse packets.

questions.question: What is the name of the POSIX-recommended suffix for typedef data type names?

options.B: _t

questions.explanation: POSIX recommends (and reserves) the _t suffix for typedef'd type names (e.g., queue_t, size_t, uint8_t). This distinguishes type names from variables and function names.

questions.question: In the KBC 'Command Byte', what does the DIS bit (bit 4) do when set?

options.B: Disables the keyboard interface (by driving the clock line low).

questions.explanation: The DIS bit (bit 4) in the KBC Command Byte, when set to 1, disables the keyboard interface by driving the clock line low, preventing the keyboard from sending data. This is used during polling when you need exclusive access.

questions.question: What is the difference between the XResolution and BytesPerScanLine fields in vbe_mode_info_t?

options.B: XResolution is in pixels; BytesPerScanLine is in bytes and may include padding.
options.C: XResolution is the physical width; BytesPerScanLine is the virtual width.
options.D: XResolution applies only to text modes.

questions.explanation: XResolution is the horizontal resolution in pixels. BytesPerScanLine is the number of bytes from one scan line to the next — it is XResolution * bytes_per_pixel, possibly rounded up for alignment. Always use BytesPerScanLine for row offsets.

questions.question: In the OOP-in-C pattern, if the struct definition is hidden in the .c file and only a forward declaration appears in the .h file, what operations are restricted for users of the class?

options.A: Users cannot call any functions on the object.
options.B: Users cannot directly access struct members; they can only hold pointers and call provided functions.
options.C: Users cannot create pointers to the type.

questions.explanation: An incomplete type (forward-declared struct) allows pointers to be declared, but the compiler cannot compute sizeof() or access any fields. This enforces encapsulation: users must go through the provided API functions.

questions.question: Which assembly instruction is used by an interrupt handler to return from an interrupt?

options.B: IRETD

questions.explanation: IRETD (Interrupt Return Doubleword) is the x86 instruction used to return from a 32-bit interrupt handler. It restores CS, EIP, and EFLAGS from the stack, returning to the interrupted code.

questions.question: In the context of Lab 5's video_test_move(), what are the two approaches to avoid sprite trails?

options.A: Use a higher frame rate OR use hardware sprite registers.
options.B: Erase previous position and redraw OR use double buffering (draw on back buffer, copy to screen).
options.D: Use V-sync OR use triple buffering.

questions.explanation: Alternative 1: Before drawing at the new position, reset the pixels at the old position (erase). Alternative 2: Redraw the entire frame on a back buffer each tick, then copy to the frame buffer (double buffering).

questions.question: How can you read the current count of a timer without disturbing it?

options.B: Use the Read-Back command written to port 0x43, requesting COUNT for the desired timer, then read from the timer's port.
options.C: Call sys_irqsetpolicy() to pause the counter.
options.D: Write a latch command to port 0x43 and then read from the timer port.

questions.explanation: The Read-Back command (or Counter Latch command) freezes the current count into a latch, allowing you to read it without the counter changing mid-read. You then read from the timer's data port (e.g., 0x40 for Timer 0).

questions.question: What C concept is similar to C++ templates for implementing a generic (type-independent) data structure?

options.B: void* pointers

questions.explanation: void* is a generic pointer that can point to any type. By using void* and storing the element size (el_size), you can implement type-independent containers like gqueue — similar to how C++ templates work but without type safety.

questions.question: In C, what does int n = 12 ^ 10; evaluate to?
What is the output of printf("%x", 255) in C?

options.B: ff

questions.explanation: %x prints the integer in lowercase hexadecimal without a prefix. 255 = 0xFF, so the output is 'ff'.

questions.question: What is the interrupt vector for IRQ 0 (Timer 0)?
questions.explanation: IRQ 0 maps to vector 0x08. IRQ 1 (keyboard) → 0x09. IRQ 8 (RTC) → 0x70. These mappings were specified by IBM and can be reconfigured at boot time.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the two-byte scancode prefix 0xE0?

options.B: It introduces an extended scancode for keys added after the original PC keyboard (arrow keys, function keys, etc.).
options.C: It indicates a modifier key (Shift, Ctrl, Alt).
options.D: It resets the keyboard scancode counter.

questions.explanation: 0xE0 is the escape byte for extended scancodes. Keys not present on the original PC (e.g., arrow keys, Insert, Delete, NumPad Enter) send 0xE0 followed by a second byte as their scancode.

questions.question: In what mode does the PS/2 mouse only send data when explicitly requested by the host?

options.B: Remote mode

questions.explanation: In remote mode, the mouse sends data only in response to a Read Data (0xEB) command from the host. Stream mode is the default where data is sent automatically on events.

questions.question: In VBE mode 0x105 (1024×768 @ 8-bit indexed), writing buffer[47] = 0x2 — what does this address correspond to on a 10×10 screen with 2 colour channels per pixel?

options.B: Channel 1 of pixel (x=3, y=2)
options.D: It is an invalid address for this mode.

questions.explanation: With 2 bytes/pixel, pixel (x, y) starts at offset (y * 10 + x) * 2. For Green channel (index 1): offset = (y*10 + x)*2 + 1. For (x=2, y=3): 3*10*2 + 2*2 + 1 = 60 + 4 + 1 = 65 → buffer[65]. For buffer[47] with Green: (47-1)/2 = 23 → pixel 23 → x=3, y=2, channel 1.

questions.question: How many elements does a simplified XPM C array have (for an image with W columns, H rows, and N colours)?

options.B: 1 + N + H

questions.explanation: The XPM array has: 1 header string ('W H N 1') + N colour definition strings + H image row strings = 1 + N + H total elements.

questions.question: In C, what does the statement 'int n = 1 << 3;' produce?
questions.explanation: 1 << 3 shifts the bit pattern 0001 left by 3 positions: 1000 = 8.

questions.question: What is the time format used by the RTC by default on most PCs?

options.B: BCD, 24-hour

questions.explanation: By default, most PC RTCs store time in BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal) format and use 24-hour time. The DM and 24/12 bits in REGISTER_B control the format, but you should not change them to avoid OS conflicts.

questions.question: In the generic queue (gqueue), the put function uses: memcpy(q->buf + q->in * q->el_size, el, q->el_size). What does 'q->buf + q->in * q->el_size' represent?

options.A: The address of the queue's first element.
options.B: The byte address within the buffer where the new element should be stored.
options.C: The total size of the buffer.
options.D: The number of elements in the queue.

questions.explanation: q->buf is a void* pointer to the start of the buffer. q->in is the index of the next write position. q->el_size is bytes per element. Adding q->in * q->el_size gives the byte offset of the slot where the new element should be written.

questions.question: How does the Minix Generic Interrupt Handler know which DDs to notify when an interrupt occurs?

options.A: It reads the device vendor table.
options.B: It looks up its internal subscription list populated by sys_irqsetpolicy() calls from DDs.
options.C: It broadcasts to all running processes.
options.D: It queries the PIC for the list of interested processes.

questions.explanation: When a DD calls sys_irqsetpolicy(), the kernel records the DD's process ID and hook_id in an internal interrupt subscription table. When the interrupt fires, the GIH looks up all subscriptions for that IRQ line and notifies the registered DDs.

questions.question: What is the issue with the following C code: 'uchar port = 0x5a; if ( (~port >> 4) & 0x0f )' and how is it fixed?

options.A: There is no issue; the code is correct.
options.B: Due to integer promotion, ~port extends to 32 bits; fix: '(uint8_t)(~port) >> 4'.
options.C: The >> operator cannot be applied to uchar; cast to int first.
options.D: 0x0f should be 0xF0 for the MS nibble.

questions.explanation: ~port promotes port to int before complementing → 0xffffffa5. >> 4 → 0x0ffffffa. Checking & 0x0f gives 0x0a, not 0x0a from the expected 8-bit result 0xa5 >> 4. Fix: cast to uint8_t after complement: (uint8_t)(~port) >> 4 gives 0x0a correctly.

questions.question: In Minix 3, what kind of message causes the is_ipc_notify() function to return true?

options.A: A regular service request from another process.
options.B: A notification message — asynchronously sent by the kernel (e.g., for interrupt handling).
options.D: A broadcast message from the OS.

questions.explanation: is_ipc_notify(ipc_status) returns true when the received message is a notification (the special IPC message type used by the kernel GIH for interrupt signalling), as opposed to a standard service-request message.

questions.question: In VBE mode 0x105 (indexed colour, 8bpp), all palette indices above 63 map to which colour by default in the Minix Lab 5 setup?

options.B: Red

questions.explanation: The Minix Lab 5 VBE mode 0x105 palette is initialised with only 64 colours (indices 0–63). Indices 64–255 are set to black (RGB 0,0,0) by default.

questions.question: What is the C standard library function to measure the length of a null-terminated string?

options.B: strlen()

questions.explanation: strlen(const char *s) returns the number of characters in the null-terminated string s, excluding the terminating '\0'. It is declared in <string.h>.

questions.question: What is an OOP object?

options.B: An instance of a class — a specific set of state data with associated behaviour (methods).
options.C: A C struct with no functions.

questions.explanation: An OOP object is an instance of a class. It has its own state (data members) and behaviour (methods). In C, each call to new_queue() creates a new queue_t object with its own state.

questions.question: In Lab 3 (kbd_test_poll), after using the KBC for polling, what must be done before exiting?

options.A: Nothing — the KBC resets automatically when the program exits.
options.B: The KBC Command Byte must be restored to re-enable keyboard interrupts.
options.C: The keyboard must be reset with command 0xFF.
options.D: The PIC mask must be restored.

questions.explanation: kbd_test_poll() disables KBC keyboard interrupts by clearing the INT bit in the Command Byte. Before exiting, the original Command Byte (with INT set) must be written back so that subsequent keyboard activity generates interrupts normally.

questions.question: What does the IRQF bit in RTC REGISTER_C indicate?

options.A: An IRQ is being generated by the RTC.
options.B: An IRQ line is being shared with another device.

questions.explanation: The IRQF bit (bit 7 of REGISTER_C) is the logical OR of all enabled and pending interrupt flags (PF & PIE, AF & AIE, UF & UIE). When IRQF=1, the RTC's IRQ line is being asserted.

questions.question: What is the difference between '==' and '=' in C?

options.A: They are identical; both are comparison operators.
options.B: '==' is the equality comparison operator; '=' is the assignment operator.

questions.explanation: In C, '=' assigns a value to a variable (e.g., x = 5). '==' tests equality and returns true (non-zero) or false (0). A common bug is writing 'if (x = 0)' (assignment) instead of 'if (x == 0)' (comparison).

questions.question: Why is driver_receive() a blocking call rather than a polling call in Minix?

options.A: Blocking calls are simpler to implement.
options.B: A blocking call allows the OS scheduler to run other processes while the DD waits, improving overall system efficiency.
options.D: It is required by the POSIX standard.

questions.explanation: When driver_receive() blocks, the DD process is removed from the run queue and the CPU is given to other processes. This avoids wasting CPU cycles in a busy-wait loop, making it far more efficient than continuous polling.

questions.question: What is the effect of using #define LAB3 and #ifdef LAB3 / cnt++; / #endif in the lab code?

options.B: It conditionally includes the counter-increment code only when LAB3 is defined (allowing the same source to be used in and out of the lab context).
options.C: It creates a compile-time error if LAB3 is not defined.
options.D: It disables optimisation for that code block.

questions.explanation: Conditional compilation (#ifdef LAB3) includes the counting code only when the preprocessor symbol LAB3 is defined (e.g., via CPPFLAGS += -D LAB3 in the Makefile). This is how you add debugging/counting code without affecting production builds.

questions.question: What must the DD do to enable the mouse in stream mode at Lab 4 start (since Minix disables it)?

options.A: Write command 0xA8 to port 0x64 to enable the mouse interface.
options.B: Call mouse_enable_data_reporting() (or send command 0xF4 to the mouse via 0xD4).
options.C: Write to REGISTER_B of the RTC.

questions.explanation: Minix disables mouse data reporting in text mode. The DD must send command 0xF4 (Enable Data Reporting) to the mouse (via KBC command 0xD4). The LCF provides mouse_enable_data_reporting() as a convenience function for this.

questions.question: What is the VGA BIOS interrupt used for video card operations?

options.B: INT 0x14

questions.explanation: INT 0x10 is the standard BIOS interrupt for video card services. Setting AH=0 selects the Set Video Mode function; AH=4Fh selects VBE functions.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Interrupt Mask Register in the PIC?

options.A: To enable/disable specific IRQ lines from reaching the CPU.
options.B: To store the address of the interrupt handler.
options.C: To count the number of interrupts received.
options.D: To set the priority of each IRQ.

questions.explanation: The Interrupt Mask Register (IMR) allows software to enable or disable specific IRQ lines. A bit set to 1 masks (disables) the corresponding IRQ. Used by the GIH and by DD calls like sys_irqdisable().

questions.question: Why is 'n << 32' undefined behaviour in C for a 32-bit int n?

options.A: Shifting by 32 creates a divide by zero.
options.B: The C standard defines behaviour only for shift amounts in [0, bit_width-1]. Shifting by 32 on a 32-bit int is undefined.
options.C: The left-shift operator does not accept 32 as an operand.

questions.explanation: The C standard specifies that the shift amount must be non-negative and less than the bit-width of the left operand. For a 32-bit int, the valid shift amounts are 0–31. Shifting by 32 is undefined behaviour.

questions.question: Which field of vbe_mode_info_t provides the physical start address of the linear frame buffer?

options.B: BitsPerPixel

questions.explanation: The PhysBasePtr field (uint32_t) in the vbe_mode_info_t struct contains the physical memory address of the start of the linear frame buffer for the given VBE mode.

questions.question: When sending a command to the KBC via port 0x64, which bit must be checked first?

options.B: IBF (Input Buffer Full)

questions.explanation: Before writing any command to port 0x64 (or data to 0x60), the IBF bit must be 0 (input buffer not full). If IBF=1, the KBC is busy processing a previous command; writing would overwrite it.

questions.question: In C, which function is used to write formatted output to standard output?

options.B: sprintf()

questions.explanation: printf() writes formatted text to standard output (stdout). It takes a format string followed by arguments corresponding to format specifiers. Declared in <stdio.h>.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes why encapsulation is beneficial in software design?

options.B: It hides implementation details, reducing coupling and making it easier to change internals without affecting clients.
options.D: It reduces the size of the compiled binary.

questions.explanation: Encapsulation hides an object's internal state and implementation from outside code. This reduces dependencies (coupling), making it possible to change the implementation (e.g., switching from a fixed array to a dynamic list) without breaking client code.

questions.question: What does the IDT (Interrupt Descriptor Table) contain?

options.A: The priorities of all IRQ lines.
options.B: A table of interrupt vectors — addresses of interrupt handlers for each vector number.
options.C: A list of all subscribed device drivers.
options.D: The contents of the PIC's IMR.

questions.explanation: The IDT is an x86 data structure indexed by interrupt vector number. Each entry (descriptor) contains the address (and privilege level) of the corresponding interrupt handler. The CPU uses the IDT to jump to the correct IH when an interrupt occurs.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'const' qualifier in C?

options.B: It declares that the variable's value should not be modified after initialisation.
options.C: It allocates the variable in read-only ROM.
options.D: It makes a variable globally accessible.

questions.explanation: const tells the compiler (and readers of the code) that the variable's value should not be modified. Attempting to modify a const variable is a compile-time error (for local variables) or undefined behaviour (for pointers to const).

questions.question: To initialise VBE mode 0x105 with the linear framebuffer enabled, which register value should be placed in BX before calling INT 0x10 with AX=0x4F02?
questions.explanation: Setting bit 14 (0x4000) of the mode number selects the linear framebuffer. 0x0105 | (1<<14) = 0x0105 | 0x4000 = 0x4105. But actually the lectures example shows: r.bx = 1<<14 | 0x105 = 0x4000 | 0x105 = 0x4105. However, the answer C (0x8105) also circulates — re-check: BIT(14) = 0x4000 → 0x4000 | 0x105 = 0x4105. Correct is B (0x4105). If the options are A=0x0105, B=0x4105, C=0x8105, D=0xC105 — the answer should be B.

questions.question: What is a race condition in the context of the RTC reading (preemption scenario in lectures)?

options.A: Two threads racing to complete a loop first.
options.B: A bug where the result of a computation depends on the unpredictable timing of multiple asynchronous accesses to shared state.
options.C: The CPU running too fast for the RTC.
options.D: Two IRQ lines competing for the same vector.

questions.explanation: A race condition occurs when two asynchronous execution flows (e.g., the main code and an interrupt handler) both access shared data, and the result depends on which one runs first. In the RTC example: if preempted while reading time registers, inconsistent values may be read.

questions.question: What port is the KBC Status Register accessible at?
questions.explanation: The KBC Status Register is at port 0x64 (read-only). Writing to 0x64 sends a KBC command. Port 0x60 is the KBC data port (Output Buffer for reads, Input Buffer for writes).

questions.question: What is the key observation about Minix 3 DD design that makes it a form of event-driven service?

options.B: DDs receive and process either interrupt notifications from the kernel OR service requests from other processes.
options.C: DDs run at kernel level and respond to system calls.

questions.explanation: A Minix 3 DD is an event-driven service: it sits in a driver_receive() loop, handling two types of events: (1) interrupt notifications (async hardware events), and (2) service requests from user processes. This is the standard Minix 3 DD architecture.

questions.question: What is the C99 boolean type and what header must be included to use it?

options.B: _Bool from <stdbool.h> (also accessible as 'bool' with the header)
options.D: C has no boolean type; use int with 0 and 1.

questions.explanation: <stdbool.h> (C99+) defines the _Bool type and the macros 'bool', 'true', and 'false'. Without this header, use int (0 = false, non-zero = true), which is the traditional C approach.

questions.question: What is raster graphics?

options.B: Images represented as a rectangular grid of pixels, each with a numeric colour value.

questions.explanation: Raster graphics represent images as a grid (matrix) of pixels, where each pixel has a colour value. Virtually all modern displays are raster displays. XPM, BMP, PNG are raster formats.

questions.question: In C, what is the data type of a function pointer that takes no arguments and returns void?

options.B: void (*fp)(void)

questions.explanation: void (*fp)(void) declares fp as a pointer to a function taking no arguments (void) and returning void. This is the type used in the event dispatch table: void (*eht[])(void).

questions.question: In Minix 3, what is a 'notification'?

options.A: A file written to disk to notify the DD.
options.B: A special kind of IPC message used by the kernel to asynchronously communicate with user-level processes.
options.D: A signal sent to a process.

questions.explanation: In Minix 3's IPC framework, a notification is a special message type. Unlike regular synchronous messages, notifications are buffered and delivered asynchronously. The GIH uses notifications to tell DDs that an interrupt has occurred.

questions.question: What is the difference between a macro definition (#define BIT(n) (1<<n)) and a function?

options.A: Macros are type-safe; functions are not.
options.B: Macros are expanded by the preprocessor (text substitution, no type checking); functions are compiled with type checking and have call overhead.
options.C: Functions can be recursive; macros cannot.
options.D: Macros are stored in ROM; functions in RAM.

questions.explanation: Macros are textual substitution by the C preprocessor — fast (no call overhead) but lack type safety and may have side effects with arguments evaluated multiple times. Functions are type-checked by the compiler.

questions.question: In the KBC command byte, what is the purpose of the INT bit (bit 0)?

options.A: It enables keyboard interrupts when OBF is set with keyboard data.
options.B: It enables mouse interrupts.

questions.explanation: The INT bit (bit 0) in the KBC Command Byte enables the KBC to generate an interrupt (IRQ 1) when the output buffer has keyboard data. INT2 (bit 1) does the same for mouse data (IRQ 12).

questions.question: What C library function copies a string including the null terminator?

options.B: strncpy()

questions.explanation: strcpy(dst, src) copies the null-terminated string src to dst, including the '\0' terminator. It assumes dst is large enough. strncpy() copies at most n characters and may not null-terminate.

questions.question: What are the RedMaskSize and RedFieldPosition fields in vbe_mode_info_t used for?

options.A: They define the screen aspect ratio.
options.B: They describe the bit position and size of the Red colour component within each pixel for direct-colour modes.
options.C: They are only used for indexed colour modes.
options.D: They specify the physical size of the screen in mm.

questions.explanation: In direct-colour modes, each pixel colour is packed into bytes. RedMaskSize gives the number of bits used for Red; RedFieldPosition gives the bit offset of the Red component within the pixel. Similar fields exist for Green, Blue, and optionally Alpha.

questions.question: The OOP property that an object's internal state can only be modified through its methods is called:

options.B: Polymorphism

questions.explanation: Data encapsulation (or data hiding) is the OOP property where the internal state of an object is accessible only via its methods (public interface). This prevents external code from directly corrupting the object's state.

questions.question: What does the C standard library function malloc(n) return?

options.A: A void* pointer to a zeroed block of n bytes on the heap.
options.B: A void* pointer to an uninitialised block of n bytes on the heap, or NULL on failure.
options.C: An int indicating success or failure.
options.D: A pointer to n bytes on the stack.

questions.explanation: malloc(n) allocates n bytes on the heap and returns a void* pointer to the block (which must be cast to the desired type). The memory is NOT initialised. Returns NULL if allocation fails.

questions.question: The irq_set mask used in 'msg.m_notify.interrupts & irq_set' is computed as:

options.B: irq_set = BIT(hook_id) — where hook_id is the value provided to (and returned by) sys_irqsetpolicy()

questions.explanation: When the DD calls sys_irqsetpolicy(irq_line, policy, &hook_id) with an input hook_id (e.g., 0), the kernel returns the actual hook_id it assigned. The DD stores irq_set = BIT(hook_id) and uses it to detect its interrupt in msg.m_notify.interrupts.

questions.question: In C, what happens if you access an array element beyond its bounds (e.g., a[5] for a 5-element array a[0..4])?

options.B: The access returns 0.
options.C: Undefined behaviour — may corrupt memory or crash.

questions.explanation: C does not perform runtime bounds checking. Accessing a[-1] or a[5] reads/writes memory adjacent to the array, which may belong to other variables or the stack. This is undefined behaviour and a common source of security vulnerabilities (buffer overflow).

questions.question: In which application domains is event-driven design commonly used?

options.B: Graphical user interfaces, games, communications/network software, and embedded systems.

questions.explanation: Event-driven design is prevalent in: (1) GUIs — responding to button clicks, mouse moves; (2) Games — handling player input and game events; (3) Network software — handling incoming packets; (4) Embedded systems — responding to sensor events. All LCOM labs use this paradigm.

questions.question: When does the KBC set the AUX bit (bit 5) of its Status Register?

options.A: When the keyboard sends a parity error.
options.B: When the byte in the output buffer came from the mouse (auxiliary device).
options.C: When the keyboard interface is inhibited.
options.D: When the input buffer is ready for a new command.

questions.explanation: The Aux bit distinguishes the source of the byte in the KBC's output buffer. Aux=0 → keyboard data; Aux=1 → mouse (auxiliary device) data. A keyboard DD should only process output buffer data when Aux=0; a mouse DD when Aux=1.

questions.question: How does disabling processor interrupts (with 'disable()') help when reading the RTC time/date registers?

options.A: It prevents the RTC from generating interrupts during the read.
options.B: It ensures no other code (including the OS's timer IH) preempts the DD between individual register reads, preventing a race condition.
options.C: It increases the speed of sys_inb() calls.
options.D: It is not helpful; only the UIP flag should be used.

questions.explanation: Disabling CPU interrupts between reads prevents any interrupt handler from preempting the DD between register reads. This ensures all time registers are read atomically, so they all belong to the same time value — no mid-read update corruption.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'CPPFLAGS += -D LAB3' in a Minix Makefile?

options.A: To define a C function named LAB3.
options.B: To pass the preprocessor symbol LAB3 to the C preprocessor, enabling conditional compilation blocks guarded by #ifdef LAB3.
options.C: To link with the LAB3 library.
options.D: To set the compiler optimisation level to LAB3.

questions.explanation: -D LAB3 instructs the C preprocessor to define the symbol LAB3 as if '#define LAB3' were at the top of every source file. Code between #ifdef LAB3 and #endif is then compiled only for Lab 3 builds.

questions.question: Why does the C standard say that pointer arithmetic on void* is not allowed, yet GCC allows it with a warning?

options.A: GCC always follows the C standard strictly.
options.B: GCC has a GNU extension that treats void* arithmetic as byte-level (size 1), but this is non-portable.
options.C: The C standard allows void* arithmetic in C99.
options.D: GCC converts void* to char* internally.

questions.explanation: GCC implements a non-standard extension where void* pointer arithmetic is treated as if the pointed-to element size is 1 byte. This is convenient but non-portable (MSVC, Clang-strict don't allow it). The correct portable approach is to cast to char* or uint8_t*.

questions.question: In Lab 5's indexed mode (0x105), how do you compute the colour index to use for the pattern video_test_pattern()?

options.A: index(row, col) = (first + (row * cols + col) * step) % (1 << BitsPerPixel)
options.B: index(row, col) = row + col

questions.explanation: The Lab 5 handout formula for indexed mode is: index(row, col) = (first + (row * no_rectangles + col) * step) % (1 << BitsPerPixel), cycling through palette indices.

questions.question: Which timer of the i8254 is used for Lab 5's frame rate control?

options.B: Timer 0

questions.explanation: Lab 5 uses Timer 0 interrupts (at Minix's default 60 Hz) to control the animation frame rate. The DD counts timer interrupts to advance the sprite's position at the correct speed.

questions.question: In C, what is a 'dangling pointer'?

options.A: A pointer that has never been initialised.
options.B: A pointer that points to memory that has been freed.
options.D: A pointer to a local variable that is still in scope.

questions.explanation: A dangling pointer is one that still holds the address of a memory region that has been freed (e.g., after calling free(ptr)). Dereferencing a dangling pointer is undefined behaviour.

questions.question: In a state machine for gesture detection, how does the machine determine the gesture has been completed?

options.A: By counting the total number of mouse packets received.
options.B: By following state transitions triggered by specific event sequences until a terminal (accepting) state is reached.
options.D: By checking the mouse's cumulative displacement.

questions.explanation: The state machine transitions between states in response to events. A gesture is 'detected' when the machine enters an accepting (final) state after the correct sequence of events (e.g., right button press → upward movement → right button release).

questions.question: What is structure padding in C and why does it occur?

options.A: The compiler adds extra bytes between struct members to align them to their natural alignment boundaries, improving memory access speed.
options.B: The compiler removes unused bytes from structs.
options.C: The programmer must manually add padding bytes.

questions.explanation: Most CPUs can access naturally aligned data faster (or may require alignment). To ensure each member is aligned, the compiler inserts padding bytes between members. E.g., a 4-byte int after a 1-byte char may have 3 bytes of padding. #pragma pack(1) disables this.

questions.question: What is the KBC command 0xAA used for?

options.B: Write Command Byte

questions.explanation: KBC command 0xAA (written to port 0x64) performs a self-test of the KBC. It returns 0x55 if the test passes, or 0xFC if it fails.

questions.question: In the Minix interrupt handling flow, when does the GIH issue the IRETD instruction?

options.B: After notifying DDs, sending EOI to PIC, and optionally unmasking the IRQ.
options.C: Before sending the EOI to the PIC.
options.D: Only after the DD has finished processing the interrupt.

questions.explanation: The GIH sequence: (1) mask IRQ, (2) notify DDs (via IPC notifications), (3) if IRQ_REENABLE: unmask IRQ, (4) EOI to PIC, (5) IRETD. The DD processes the interrupt asynchronously in user space after GIH returns.

questions.question: What is the PhysBasePtr field's data type in vbe_mode_info_t?

options.B: uint32_t

questions.explanation: PhysBasePtr is declared as uint32_t — a 32-bit physical address (sufficient for IA-32's 4 GB physical address space). It must be cast to a pointer type before being passed to vm_map_phys().

questions.question: In C, the -> operator is equivalent to:

options.B: &ptr.member

questions.explanation: q->count is exactly equivalent to (*q).count: first dereference the pointer q, then access the count member. The -> operator is a convenient shorthand for this common pattern.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'delete_queue()' function in OOP-in-C?

options.A: To empty all elements from the queue.
options.B: To act as a destructor: free the queue's internal buffer and then free the queue struct itself.
options.C: To reset the queue to its initial state.

questions.explanation: delete_queue(queue_t *q) is the destructor: it calls free(q->buf) to release the element buffer, then free(q) to release the queue struct. This prevents memory leaks.

questions.question: When using double buffering in Lab 5's animation, what is copied to the VRAM frame buffer at the end of each frame?

options.B: The entire back buffer (the complete next frame).

questions.explanation: With double buffering, the application draws the complete next frame on a back buffer in RAM. At the appropriate time (end of frame, during vertical retrace), the entire back buffer is copied to the VRAM frame buffer (or page-flipping changes the display start address).

questions.question: The RTC is connected to which PIC and IRQ line?

options.B: PIC 1, IRQ 8

questions.explanation: The RTC is connected to IRQ 0 of PIC 2, which corresponds to global IRQ 8 and interrupt vector 0x70. PIC 2 is cascaded through IRQ 2 of PIC 1.

questions.question: What is the typical C idiom to iterate through all elements of an array of N integers using pointer arithmetic?

options.A: for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) { a[i] = 0; }
options.B: for (int *p = a; p < a + N; p++) { *p = 0; }
options.C: Both A and B are correct idioms.
options.D: Neither; arrays cannot be iterated with pointers.

questions.explanation: Both idioms are correct in C. Option A uses index notation; Option B uses pointer arithmetic (p++ advances by sizeof(int) bytes). Both are seen in LCOM code — e.g., vg_fill() uses pointer arithmetic.

questions.question: When programming the i8254 Timer 0 with LSB then MSB, what is the order of writes to port 0x40?

options.B: Write LSB first (low byte), then MSB (high byte).
options.D: Write the entire 16-bit value in one write.

questions.explanation: When the control word selects 'LSB followed by MSB' (bits 5–4 = 11), the initial count must be written as two consecutive byte writes to the timer port: first the low byte (LSB), then the high byte (MSB).

questions.question: What is the state of the Left Button bit (L.B., bit 0 of byte 1) in a mouse packet when the left button is pressed?
questions.explanation: In the PS/2 mouse data packet, bit 0 of byte 1 is the Left Button state: 1 if pressed, 0 if released. Similarly, bit 1 is the Right Button, bit 2 is the Middle Button.

questions.question: What does #include <stdlib.h> provide in C?

options.B: I/O functions like printf and scanf.
options.C: General utilities: malloc, free, realloc, rand, exit, etc.

questions.explanation: <stdlib.h> provides general-purpose functions including memory management (malloc, free, realloc, calloc), random number generation (rand, srand), process control (exit), and integer/string conversion (atoi, strtoul).

questions.question: Which function resets the video card back to text mode in Minix Lab 5?

options.A: vg_exit() — calls BIOS INT 10h with AH=0, AL=3
options.B: vm_unmap_phys()

questions.explanation: vg_exit() calls sys_int86 with BIOS INT 10h, AH=0 (Set Video Mode), AL=3 (text mode: 25 lines × 80 columns, 16 colours). This restores the text mode expected by Minix.

questions.question: In the animated sprite (AnimSprite) class, what does asp->aspeed represent?

options.A: The number of pixels the sprite moves per frame.
options.B: The number of frames each pixmap is displayed before switching to the next one.
options.C: The total number of animation frames.

questions.explanation: aspeed (animation speed) in the AnimSprite class is the number of rendering frames each XPM pixmap is displayed before the animation advances to the next XPM. A smaller aspeed = faster animation.

questions.question: Which of the following is a correct way to declare a struct in C?

options.B: struct queue { int buf[10]; };

questions.explanation: In C, struct queue { int buf[10]; }; declares a struct named 'queue' with one member. To use it: struct queue q; or after typedef: queue_t q;. 'class' is a C++ keyword not available in C.

questions.question: What is the recommended approach in Minix to handle an interrupt that occurred 'in the past' (e.g., before the interrupt loop started)?

options.A: Ignore it; the interrupt will fire again.
options.B: Read REGISTER_C of the RTC or the relevant status register to clear any pending interrupt flag before entering the interrupt loop.

questions.explanation: If a previous run of the program left a pending interrupt unacknowledged, the interrupt may fire immediately when you enter the loop. The fix: before the loop, read (and discard) the relevant status register — e.g., read RTC REGISTER_C to clear all RTC interrupt flags.

questions.question: In C, what is the result of sizeof(int *) on a 32-bit architecture?

options.B: 4 bytes
options.D: It depends on the type pointed to.

questions.explanation: On a 32-bit architecture, all pointer types have the same size: 4 bytes (32 bits), regardless of the type they point to. On 64-bit architectures, pointers are 8 bytes. sizeof(int*) == sizeof(void*) == sizeof(char*) on any given platform.
